task_context.question: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?
task_context.agent_role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

visible_agent_inputs.question: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?
visible_agent_inputs.summary_1:
Primary topics: Peter Frampton; Rod Stewart — 1976 Hot 100 songs and albums.
- Peter Frampton: "'Show Me the Way' ... gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album 'Frampton Comes Alive!'"; reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100. (passage [4])
- Rod Stewart: "'Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)' ... for his 1976 album 'A Night on the Town'"; became his second US chart topper on the "Billboard" Hot 100, spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977. (passage [6])
- Missing bridge fact: whether the album (possible bridge entity: "Frampton Comes Alive!" for Peter Frampton; possible bridge entity: "A Night on the Town" for Rod Stewart) "reflects on his depressive state" — this thematic relation is not stated in the passages and is unverified for each candidate.
Next-hop retrieval directives (priority order):
1) Open "Frampton Comes Alive!" Wikipedia page — inspect lede/infobox, "Background", "Composition"/"Themes" and "Reception" sections for any statement that the album reflects Frampton's depressive state.
2) Open "A Night on the Town" (Rod Stewart album) page — inspect lede/infobox, "Background"/"Recording" or "Themes" sections for any statement that the album reflects Stewart's depressive state.
3) If neither page confirms, search artist+album with tight queries: "Peter Frampton 'depress*' 'Frampton Comes Alive!'", and "Rod Stewart 'depress*' 'A Night on the Town'" (look for interviews, album analyses, or critical reviews discussing depressive themes).

current_output.query: Frampton Comes Alive A Night on the Town depression

input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Priority rules for ambiguity and disambiguation:
- If summary_1 lists multiple candidate anchors or otherwise signals ambiguity, emit a single connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens (use the order or priority visible in summary_1) and then append exactly one short attribute token or a visible year token for disambiguation.
- If summary_1 contains a comma-separated list of individuals and the missing evidence concerns a member, prefer the earliest-listed individual token paired with the visible group token and append the single attribute token member.
- If the question contains a visible year token that disambiguates a title, include that year token before the attribute token in the query.
- If none of the above applies, emit a single-anchor query using the most relevant visible token and at most one short attribute token when the question implies an attribute.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.

output.query: George Harrison Extra Texture Billboard Hot 100 1976
Attitudes (band) Extra Texture (Read All About It) Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?

retrieved_doc_snippets: Attitudes (band) | Attitudes was a rock band from Los Angeles consisting of keyboardist David Foster, guitarist Danny Kortchmar, bass guitarist Paul Stallworth and drummer Jim Keltner. Its four members came together while working as session musicians during the recording of George Harrison's album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)". One single by the band, "Sweet Summer Music" reached #94 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 in 1976. James Taylor later covered their single "Honey Don't Leave L.A." and th ...[truncated]

Honey Don't Leave L.A. | "Honey Don't Leave L.A." is a song written by Danny Kortchmar. It was first recorded by Attitudes, a band consisting of Kortchmar, Paul Stallworth, Jim Keltner and David Foster, who met while backing George Harrison on his album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)". It was released on Attitudes' self-titled debut album in 1976. It was also released as a promotional single on Harrison's Dark Horse Records label.

Extra Texture (Read All About It) | Extra Texture (Read All About It) is the sixth studio album by English musician George Harrison, released in September 1975. It was Harrison's final album under his contract with Apple Records and EMI, and the last studio album issued by Apple. The release came nine months after his troubled 1974 North American tour with Ravi Shankar and the poorly received "Dark Horse" album. The melancholic mood of the recording reflects Harrison's depressed state at the cri ...[truncated]

You (George Harrison song) | "You" is a song by English musician George Harrison, released as the opening track of his 1975 album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)". It was also the album's lead single, becoming a top 20 hit in America and reaching number 9 in Canada. A 45-second instrumental portion of the song, titled "A Bit More of You", appears on "Extra Texture" also, opening side two of the original LP format. Harrison wrote "You" in 1970 as a song for Ronnie Spector, formerly of the Rone ...[truncated]

Tired of Midnight Blue | "Tired of Midnight Blue" is a song by English musician George Harrison, released on his 1975 album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)". It was written after a night out with music-industry executives in Los Angeles – an event that Harrison found particularly depressing. The recording includes contributions from Leon Russell, on piano, and Jim Keltner, who plays drums and percussion.

Can't Stop Thinking About You | "Can't Stop Thinking About You" is a song by English musician George Harrison, released in 1975 on his final album for Apple Records, "Extra Texture (Read All About It)". A love song in the style of a soul/R&B ballad, it was written by Harrison in December 1973, towards the end of his marriage to Pattie Boyd and while he was having an affair with Maureen Starkey, the wife of his former Beatles bandmate Ringo Starr. Having first considered the song for his 1974 rel ...[truncated]

World of Stone | "World of Stone" is a song by English rock musician George Harrison, released in 1975 on "Extra Texture (Read All About It)", his final album for Apple Records. It was also issued as the B-side of the album's lead single, "You". Harrison wrote the song in 1973 but recorded it two years later, following the unfavourable critical reception afforded his 1974 North American tour and the "Dark Horse" album. Due to its context on release, commentators view "World of Stone" as a plea f ...[truncated]
